text: This fifty-five years old male presented with complaint of pain, recurrent pain in epigastric and right hypochondriac region. And from last two days before he presented to me, he was having vomiting and fever. and upon examination the Murphy's sign was positive so there was the history of total three days of the acute attack

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, encountering adhesions and thickening of the gallbladder wall. Electrocautery and a Maryland dissector are used for both blunt and sharp dissection. Hydro dissection is also employed to manage adhesions. The gallbladder is grasped proximally and pulled upwards to clarify the anatomy in the neck region, with traction applied towards the right shoulder to highlight structures in the Calot's triangle.

The surgeon maintains traction on the gallbladder to reduce the risk of injury to the common bile duct (CBD) during adhesiolysis. The Rubio sulcus is monitored to ensure dissection remains above it, particularly when ligating or clipping the cystic duct. Dense adhesions are visible around the gallbladder neck and within the Calot's triangle region.
The surgeon performs hydro dissection using a 5mm suction device, with water flow helping to delineate the anatomical planes amidst dense adhesions, reducing the risk of injury and bleeding.

The surgeon performs adenolysis at the junction of the cystic duct and gallbladder neck using the tip of a sucker to create a plane between the cystic artery and cystic duct. The cystic duct appears dilated. Electrocautery is used to address small bends not amenable to blunt dissection. The gallbladder neck is lifted above the sylovial sulcus, enhancing anatomical clarity.

The surgeon exposes Hartman's pouch and performs further adenolysis in the region. Hartman's pouch is visible above the cystic duct, which has sustained an injury due to direct contact with the tip of the hook. The surgeon clips the cystic artery to improve visibility and assesses the extent of the cystic duct injury. The distal part of the cystic artery is coagulated after clipping.

The surgeon carefully monitors the tip of the hook during electrocautery to avoid further injury, identifying a pinpoint iatrogenic injury in the middle part of the cystic duct. Using Maryland forceps and blunt dissection, the surgeon creates space in the groove of the peritoneal reflection over the right side of the cystic duct, enhancing visibility of the suspicious structure near the gallbladder fossa.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to expose the proximal length of the cystic duct, revealing a clear view of a pinpoint iatrogenic injury in the middle of the duct. An artery with pulsations and two branches is observed, running directly into the gallbladder bed and liver bed, indicating variable anatomy.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneal attachments on both medial and lateral aspects of the gallbladder, revealing a variable artery. A clip is applied to the more prominent right-sided branch, while the left branch courses deep into the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon uses hydro dissection to skeletonize a vessel, applying clips proximally and distally before cutting between them to reveal the vessel's lumen. Clips are then applied to the cystic duct: two proximal clips near the junction with the common bile duct and one distal clip near the gallbladder neck. The cystic duct is cut between the second and third clips, at the site of a previous iatrogenic injury. The gallbladder is separated from the liver bed using hydro dissection or electrocautery.

The surgeon uses electrocautery in spray mode with intermittent pulsatile current to minimize smoke production, facilitating a clear view of the dissection plane. The gallbladder is gradually freed from its final attachment to the liver bed at the fundus, completing the dissection.
The surgeon uses a grasper to remove the gallbladder through the epigastric port. A drain is then placed inside the surgical site for postoperative fluid management.
